attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: introduced types `ScenarioResult = { premium?: number; payout?: number }` and `ScenarioOutput = { results: ScenarioResult[] }`; typed `runScenario` return as `ScenarioOutput`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — make the output contract explicit at the type level so future cycles have a shared shape for both quote (`premium`) and claim (`payout`) results
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(input): ScenarioOutput`; result entries use keys `premium` and `payout`
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with a `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` lookup map (`{ sword: 115, amulet: 71 }`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — both known item premiums are now equally enumerated rather than one being an implicit ternary fallback; also lowers APP mass (13 → 11) by removing the conditional. Map will naturally extend as upcoming staff/potion/rune/moonstone tests land
11	- Naming established: module-level `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE: Record<string, number>` as the per-item-type premium lookup
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: inlined the local `premium` binding in `runScenario` into the returned object literal (`{ premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[itemType] }`); also added `staff: 93` to the lookup map (this was the Green change)
15	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the named binding was redundant because the object key `premium:` already self-documents the value; mass −1. Kept `itemType` binding because `steps[0].items[0].type` is about to be reused once surcharges/discounts land
16	- Naming established: —
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green only added `potion: 49` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Tried destructuring `steps[0]`/`items[0]` into `firstStep`/`firstItem` bindings but reverted — added two bindings with no clarity gain over the direct `steps[0].items[0].type` access. Extracting the implicit `base * 1.1 + 5` formula behind the cached premiums would be premature: upcoming loyalty-discount tests will replace the `* 1.1` first-contract surcharge, and only then will the fee/surcharge split become observable. Tightening `Step.op: string` to a literal union would speculate on the claim-op shape. Function stays at ~8 mass, fully justified by 4 passing tests.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added `rune: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Rune is the first test where rounding becomes implicit (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → ceil → 28, then +5 = 33), giving the first weak signal that a `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` formula extraction with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map is coming. Holding the extraction because: (a) `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all satisfy this lone non-integer case — no test yet forces ceil specifically; (b) the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" todo (staff + loyalty + first = 76G from 70.4) will force ceil unambiguously and will land alongside the loyalty discount, at which point extracting base+formula+rounding together is one coherent change instead of two speculative ones. Naming/binding/element review unchanged from cycle 4.
23

content.text:
All 6 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `runScenario` — still fits; still returns a `ScenarioOutput` and the function will iterate steps eventually. Keep.
- `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — still describes the lookup. Keep.
- `itemType` binding — still accurate. Keep.

## Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass

```
function runScenario(input: unknown): ScenarioOutput {
  const { steps } = input as Input;
  const itemType = steps[0].items[0].type;
  return { results: [{ premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[itemType] }] };
}
```

Component count:
- Constants: 2 (`0`, `0`) = 2
- Bindings: 4 (`input`, `steps`, `itemType`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` reference) = 4
- Invocations: ~2 (property/index accesses; counting `.items[0]`, `.type` chain as accesses — being conservative, 2 invocations) = 4
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0

Total mass: ~10.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass — ✓ all 6 green.

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
The Green change added `moonstone: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Now we have two entries with the same value (`rune: 33, moonstone: 33`) but the test description explicitly says "same as rune" — the two items are **conceptually** distinct (components per the spec, with shared base cost 25G). The duplication of the value `33` is observable but encoding the relationship would be premature — see Rule 3 below.

The architecture-notes from cycle 5 explicitly flagged that the formula `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` and a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map are the next coherent extraction, deferred until the loyalty-discount + "round UP in MHPCO's favor" test makes ceil unambiguous. That logic still holds — moonstone is a second 33G data point but rounds the same way as rune (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → 28 → +5 = 33), so `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all still satisfy. No new test forces ceil.

### Rule 3: No Duplication
The value `33` now appears twice in `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Two options to remove the literal duplication:

**Option A**: Group components: `const COMPONENT_PREMIUM = 33; { rune: COMPONENT_PREMIUM, moonstone: COMPONENT_PREMIUM, ... }`. This encodes "these two share a category" but speculates on the category abstraction — runes will soon get block pricing (3 runes = 60G building block, but moonstone explicitly does NOT combine with rune per todo line 100). So "component" is the right *category* label for shared base-25G items, but blocks behave per-item-type. Naming this constant `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` is honest to the spec.

**Option B**: Extract the formula `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map. This removes the duplication of `33` *and* the implicit duplication of `* 1.1 + 5` baked into every cached premium. But — as cycle 4/5 notes explained — without loyalty discount visible we can't yet split fee from surcharge, and `Math.ceil` isn't forced.

Option A is the narrower, less speculative change that responds specifically to the new duplication moonstone introduced. Let me apply it.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring (Option A)

content.text: Tests still green. Mark Test 6 as completed.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Module-level changes only — `runScenario` body is unchanged so its mass stays at ~10.

Module-level lookup adds:
- Constant: 1 (literal `33`) = 1
- Binding: 1 (`COMPONENT_PREMIUM`) = 1
- Removes: 1 duplicate literal `33` = −1

Net module mass change: +1. Tiny rise in count, but the *intent* of "rune and moonstone share the component price" is now explicit in the code, not a coincidence of two equal literals.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted `COMPONENT_PREMIUM = 33` to name the shared price of rune and moonstone (Rule 2: reveals intent; partial Rule 3: removes literal duplication of `33`)
- Mass change: ~10 → ~11 (module-level +1) — accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP
- The name documents the *category* (component items) per the spec, anticipating that future tests (lines 96-100) will distinguish component-block pricing from individual pricing

**What was NOT done and why**:
- Did NOT extract `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` + `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE`: cycle 5 architecture note still applies — without loyalty discount or the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" test visible, neither the fee/surcharge split nor `Math.ceil` specifically is forced. Premature.
- Did NOT change types: no new shape signal from this test.

## Step 7: Append to Architecture Notes

- No refactoring (justified): Green added `rune: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Rune is the first test where rounding becomes implicit (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → ceil → 28, then +5 = 33), giving the first weak signal that a `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` formula extraction with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map is coming. Holding the extraction because: (a) `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all satisfy this lone non-integer case — no test yet forces ceil specifically; (b) the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" todo (staff + loyalty + first = 76G from 70.4) will force ceil unambiguously and will land alongside the loyalty discount, at which point extracting base+formula+rounding together is one coherent change instead of two speculative ones. ## After cycle 5
- No refactoring (justified): Green added `rune: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Rune is the first test where rounding becomes implicit (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → ceil → 28, then +5 = 33), giving the first weak signal that a `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` formula extraction with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map is coming. Holding the extraction because: (a) `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all satisfy this lone non-integer case — no test yet forces ceil specifically; (b) the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" todo (staff + loyalty + first = 76G from 70.4) will force ceil unambiguously and will land alongside the loyalty discount, at which point extracting base+formula+rounding together is one coherent change instead of two speculative ones. Naming/binding/element review unchanged from cycle 4.

## After cycle 6
- Change: extracted `const COMPONENT_PREMIUM = 33` and referenced it from both `rune` and `moonstone` entries in `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) + partial Rule 3 (literal duplication) — moonstone is the second 33G entry, and the spec calls rune/moonstone "components" sharing base 25G. Naming the shared value documents the category rather than leaving it a coincidence of two equal literals. Anticipates upcoming component-block tests (lines 96-100) that will distinguish component pricing further. Module-level mass +1; accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` + `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — cycle 5 reasoning unchanged (ceil not yet forced; loyalty/fee split not yet observable).
- Naming established: module-level `COMPONENT_PREMIUM: number` as the shared per-unit premium for component items (rune, moonstone, …)
